content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 12 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2CO_012_page_001.jpg

1. It is necessary to boast — [though it is] not profitable — but I will come to visions and revelations of [the] Lord.

2. I know a person in Christos, fourteen years ago — whether in [the] body I do not know, or outside the body I do not know, God knows — such a [one] was caught up to [the] third heaven.

3. And I know such a person — whether in [the] body or apart from the body I do not know, God knows —

4. that he was caught up into the paradise and heard unspeakable words which it is not lawful for a person to speak.

5. On behalf of such a [one] I will boast, but on behalf of myself I will not boast except in the weaknesses [of me].

6. For if I should wish to boast, I will not be foolish, for I will speak truth. But I refrain, lest anyone should credit to me beyond what he sees [in] me or hears from me.

7. And because of the surpassing [greatness] of the revelations, therefore, that I might not be exalted, there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, a messenger of Satanas, that it might strike me, that I might not be exalted.

8. Concerning this, three times I called upon the Lord that it might depart from me.

9. And he said to me, "Sufficient for you [is] the grace of me; for the power is perfected in weakness." Most gladly therefore I will rather boast in the weaknesses of me, that the power of the Christos may dwell upon me.

10. Therefore I am well-pleased in weaknesses, in insults, in necessities, in persecutions, in distresses, on behalf of Christos; for when I am weak, then I am powerful.

11. I have become foolish — you compelled me. For I ought to have been commended by you. For in nothing was I less than the super-apostles, even if I am nothing.

12. Indeed the signs of the apostle were worked among you in all endurance, both signs and wonders and powers.

13. For in what were you made less than the rest of the assemblies, except that I myself was not a burden to you? Forgive me this wrong!

14. Behold, this third [time] I am ready to come to you, and I will not be a burden. For I do not seek the [things] of you but you. For the children ought not to store up for the parents, but the parents for the children.

15. And I will most gladly spend and be spent for the souls of you, even if loving you more abundantly, I am loved less.

16. But let it be, I did not burden you, but being crafty, I took you by guile!

17. Did I take advantage of you through any of the [ones] I sent to you?

18. I urged Titos and sent the brother with [him]. Did Titos take advantage of you? Did we not walk in the same spirit? [Did we] not [walk] in the same steps?

19. For a long [time] you have been thinking that we are defending ourselves to you. Before God in Christos we speak; and all [things], beloved, [are] for the building up of you.

20. For I fear lest coming I may not find you such as I wish, and I may be found by you such as you do not wish — lest [there be] strife, jealousy, anger, selfish ambitions, slanders, gossip, puffed-up [ones], disorders;

21. lest when I come again the God of me may humble me before you, and I may mourn [for] many of the [ones] having previously sinned and not having repented of the uncleanness and sexual immorality and sensuality which they practiced.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.2 — "third heaven":** τρίτου οὐρανοῦ — in Jewish cosmology, the highest heaven (cf. 2 Enoch, Testament of Levi). Paul refers to himself in the third person, maintaining distance from this experience.

2. **v.4 — "paradise":** παράδεισος — a Persian loanword (pairidaeza, "enclosed garden"). In Jewish tradition, paradise is the abode of the righteous dead and the eschatological garden of God.

3. **v.7 — "thorn in the flesh":** σκόλοψ τῇ σαρκί — σκόλοψ means "stake/thorn/splinter." The nature of Paul's thorn is unknown: proposed identifications include illness (malaria, epilepsy, eye disease), opposition, or a spiritual affliction.

4. **v.7 — "messenger of Satanas":** ἄγγελος Σατανᾶ — the thorn is personified as Satan's emissary. Paul sees his affliction as both divinely permitted and satanically executed — a theology of suffering that holds both realities in tension.

5. **v.9 — "power is perfected in weakness":** ἡ δύναμις ἐν ἀσθενείᾳ τελεῖται — the theological thesis of the entire letter. God's power reaches its full expression precisely in human weakness.

6. **v.10 — "when I am weak, then I am powerful":** ὅταν ἀσθενῶ, τότε δυνατός εἰμι — the paradox that defines Pauline apostleship and christology.

— EMET, AI Translator
